quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rutherford model"?

options.text: Strong attraction and permanent magnetization; magnetic domains align cooperatively; responsible for permanent magnets
Gold foil experiment: most alpha particles pass through, some scatter greatly → atom is mostly empty space with a tiny dense positive nucleus
Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted
Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rutherford model" is defined as: Gold foil experiment: most alpha particles pass through, some scatter greatly → atom is mostly empty space with a tiny dense positive nucleus. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Bohr model of hydrogen"?

options.text: Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly
A quantum of electromagnetic radiation; energy E = hf; momentum p = h/λ = hf/c; no rest mass
Electrons in quantized circular orbits: E_n = -13.6/n² eV; r_n = n² × 0.0529 nm; explains hydrogen spectral lines
V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁; ideal transformers conserve power: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂; stepping up voltage steps down current

quiz_questions.explanation: "Bohr model of hydrogen" is defined as: Electrons in quantized circular orbits: E_n = -13.6/n² eV; r_n = n² × 0.0529 nm; explains hydrogen spectral lines. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Quantization condition"?

options.text: Angular momentum L = nℏ (n = 1,2,3,...); equivalently, nλ = 2πr (standing wave condition for electron orbit)
θ_incident = θ_reflected; both angles measured from the normal to the surface
Induced currents in bulk conductors; dissipate energy as heat; used in magnetic braking and induction heating
Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe

quiz_questions.explanation: "Quantization condition" is defined as: Angular momentum L = nℏ (n = 1,2,3,...); equivalently, nλ = 2πr (standing wave condition for electron orbit). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ground state vs. excited state"?

options.text: When a driving frequency matches a natural frequency of the tube, standing waves form and the sound is amplified dramatically
Restricting wave oscillations to one plane; only transverse waves can be polarized (proves light is transverse)
V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C)
Ground state: lowest energy (n=1); excited states: n > 1; atoms absorb energy to reach excited states, emit to return

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ground state vs. excited state" is defined as: Ground state: lowest energy (n=1); excited states: n > 1; atoms absorb energy to reach excited states, emit to return. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Quantum mechanical model"?

options.text: Electrons are described by wavefunctions (probability amplitudes); orbitals are regions of high probability, not definite paths
Diagnostic imaging (gamma cameras, PET) and therapy (targeted radiation, brachytherapy) using radioactive isotopes
Central maximum is widest; minima at a sin θ = mλ (m ≠ 0); a = slit width; narrower slit → wider pattern
Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted

quiz_questions.explanation: "Quantum mechanical model" is defined as: Electrons are described by wavefunctions (probability amplitudes); orbitals are regions of high probability, not definite paths. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Gold foil experiment: most alpha particles pass through, some scatter greatly → atom is mostly empty space with a tiny dense positive nucleus"?

options.text: Rydberg formula
Photon
Wave interference
Rutherford model

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rutherford model".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Electrons in quantized circular orbits: E_n = -13.6/n² eV; r_n = n² × 0.0529 nm; explains hydrogen spectral lines"?

options.text: Heisenberg uncertainty principle
Nuclear fusion
Bohr model of hydrogen
Beats

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Bohr model of hydrogen".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Angular momentum L = nℏ (n = 1,2,3,...); equivalently, nλ = 2πr (standing wave condition for electron orbit)"?

options.text: Self-inductance
Quantization condition
Quantum superposition
Superconductivity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Quantization condition".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Ground state: lowest energy (n=1); excited states: n > 1; atoms absorb energy to reach excited states, emit to return"?

options.text: De Broglie wavelength
Magnetic flux
Thin-film interference
Ground state vs. excited state

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ground state vs. excited state".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Electrons are described by wavefunctions (probability amplitudes); orbitals are regions of high probability, not definite paths"?

options.text: Magnification
Lens power
Quantum mechanical model
Quantum superposition

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Quantum mechanical model".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Electrons and Atomic Models is accurate?

options.text: The Carnot efficiency η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H sets the theoretical maximum efficiency.
The speed of sound depends on the medium's elastic and inertial properties: v = √(B/ρ) for fluids and v ≈ 331 + 0.6T m/s in air (T in °C).
Key properties: wavelength (λ), frequency (f), amplitude (A), and wave speed (v = fλ).
Electrons and Atomic Models traces the evolution of atomic theory from Thomson's plum pudding model to Bohr's quantized orbits to the quantum mechanical model.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrons and Atomic Models as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Electrons and Atomic Models?

options.text: Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.
Rutherford's gold foil experiment proved atoms have tiny, dense, positive nuclei.
Lenses use refraction to form images.
Energy carried by waves is proportional to amplitude squared: E ∝ A².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrons and Atomic Models as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Electrons and Atomic Models?

options.text: At the molecular level, temperature is proportional to average kinetic energy: ½m⟨v²⟩ = (3/2)kT.
The second law states that natural processes increase the total entropy of the universe; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold, never the reverse, without external work.
Bohr's model: electrons orbit in quantized energy levels with angular momentum L = nℏ.
Radioactive decay follows N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), with half-life t₁/₂ = ln(2)/λ.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrons and Atomic Models as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Electrons and Atomic Models?

options.text: Applications of Quantum Ideas extends quantum principles to technologies and fundamental physics.
The standing-wave condition (electron wavelength must fit the orbital circumference: nλ = 2πr) explains quantization.
Capacitors and Dielectrics store electrical energy in an electric field.
Power dissipated: P = IV = I²R = V²/R.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrons and Atomic Models as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Electrons and Atomic Models?

options.text: Photons and Quantum Mechanics introduces the quantized nature of light and energy.
Applications of Quantum Ideas extends quantum principles to technologies and fundamental physics.
Beta-minus decay (β⁻): neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino (Z→Z+1).
The modern quantum model replaces orbits with probability clouds (orbitals) described by wave functions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electrons and Atomic Models as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Electrons and Atomic Models?

options.text: Noise-canceling headphones use destructive interference: a microphone detects ambient sound, and speakers produce the inverted wave to cancel noise
The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Rutherford's experiment was done with gold because it's malleable enough to make foils only a few atoms thick—thicker foils would scatter too many alpha particles
Wireless charging (Qi standard): a changing magnetic field in the charging pad induces an EMF in the receiving coil inside the phone

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrons and Atomic Models.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Electrons and Atomic Models?

options.text: Nuclear weapons: a 20-kiloton bomb converts only about 1 gram of matter to energy (E = mc² = 0.001 kg × 9 × 10¹⁶ = 9 × 10¹³ J ≈ 20 kilotons of TNT)
Hydrogen fuel cells exploit the well-understood quantum energy levels of hydrogen for efficient energy conversion
Noise barriers on highways: effective at blocking high-frequency (short λ) sound but less effective for low-frequency (long λ) sound that diffracts over the top
Tire pressure monitoring: as tires heat up during driving, Gay-Lussac's law predicts pressure increases at constant volume—tire pressure sensors alert drivers to dangerous changes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrons and Atomic Models.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Electrons and Atomic Models applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Car side mirrors are convex ('objects in mirror are closer than they appear') to provide a wider field of view, reducing blind spots
Atomic clocks use the precise frequency of electron transitions in cesium atoms (9.19 GHz) to define the second—accurate to 1 part in 10¹⁴
Nuclear medicine: technetium-99m (a metastable nuclear isomer) is the most widely used medical radioisotope, emitting gamma rays for diagnostic imaging of organs
The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrons and Atomic Models.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Electrons and Atomic Models?

options.text: Rutherford's experiment was done with gold because it's malleable enough to make foils only a few atoms thick—thicker foils would scatter too many alpha particles
3D movies use two differently polarized images projected simultaneously; polarized glasses filter one image to each eye, creating stereoscopic depth perception
Cameras: converging lenses focus light from objects at varying distances onto the film/sensor by adjusting the lens-to-sensor distance (focusing)
Arrow of time: entropy increase explains why ice melts in warm rooms but warm rooms don't spontaneously freeze—the second law governs the direction of all natural processes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrons and Atomic Models.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Electrons and Atomic Models in practice?

options.text: Electric motors: current-carrying coils in a magnetic field experience torque (F = IL × B), converting electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy
Smoke detectors: americium-241 emits alpha particles that ionize air, creating a current; smoke disrupts the current, triggering the alarm
Hydrogen fuel cells exploit the well-understood quantum energy levels of hydrogen for efficient energy conversion
Phone chargers contain transformers (and rectifiers) that convert 120V AC to 5-20V DC for safe device charging

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electrons and Atomic Models.
